data.title: NOR Flash Next in AI-Driven Memory Crunch
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

After DRAM and NAND flash shortages, a supply crisis is brewing in another memory segment, and AI is the key driver behind the NOR flash supply crunch as well. Demand for NOR flash in AI servers is rising sharply; the number of NOR devices per server rack has increased to more than 30 units, up from roughly three to five previously.

Taiwan’s Commercial Times reports that in Nvidia’s GB200 NVL72 system, NOR content per rack already exceeds $600 and could reach $900 within two years. As a result, this surge is intensifying competition for NOR production capacity between embedded applications and AI servers.

NOR flash offers fast random-access read speeds in demanding execute-in-place applications as well as high reliability and functionality at extreme temperatures. That makes it a staple in applications where code storage is crucial, including automotive, cloud computing, and industrial systems. NOR’s high write endurance also makes it suitable for applications such as over-the-air (OTA) programs, which undergo multiple updates.

According to Taiwan’s Economic Daily News, Macronix, the world’s largest NOR flash supplier, could announce a 30% price hike for its NOR flash chips in the first quarter of 2026. Though it hasn’t happened yet, the Hsinchu, Taiwan-based memory supplier has reportedly scaled down some of its NOR capacity to increase its MLC NAND flash production.

To understand the imminent supply crisis in the NOR flash industry, it’s important to know how NOR flash devices are consumed in AI systems. And how AI applications are competing for NOR capacity with other segments such as automotive, consumer, industrial, and IoT.

NOR flash in AI

Where does NOR flash stand in the AI scheme of things? AI data center components, including network interface cards (NICs), controllers, and accelerator boards, benefit from NOR’s stability features, such as error correction code (ECC), cyclic redundancy check (CRC), continuous read support, and wrap-based data patterns that align with modern caching architectures.

NOR flash is increasingly used in AI servers and data centers as a reliable, low-latency storage solution for firmware, boot code, and system initialization. It integrates well with graphics-driven solutions, which makes it highly suitable for code storage and other AI-driven workloads.

NOR flash is critical for the safe boot and initialization processes of AI servers and high-performance computing (HPC) systems. Next, NOR flash provides fast, reliable access to firmware and underlying instructions. Then, there is the high-bandwidth memory (HBM) factor: NOR flash provides the independent power management and initialization required for each DRAM layer in HBM devices paired with AI processors.

In edge AI, NOR flash is crucial for storing operating systems and code in AI-enabled IoT, automotive, and industrial devices, where data integrity is vital. In fact, many edge SoCs are incorporating NOR for model storage because it offers deterministic read latencies. And many edge AI modules stream neural network weights directly from NOR flash for inference workloads.

The rise of AI has also exposed the limitations of 2D NOR flash, as AI applications demand ever higher densities while still requiring NOR’s high reliability. Traditional 2D NOR flash, while effective in many AI applications, is approaching physical and performance limits because its planar architecture constrains scalability toward higher-density, lower-latency memory solutions.

Enter 3D NOR flash.

3D NOR on the horizon

3D NOR flash stacks memory cells vertically to overcome the scalability issues inherent in 2D NOR’s planar architecture. Vertical stacking, which enables higher storage densities within the same footprint, reduces the need for multiple storage devices such as eMMC and NAND. 3D NOR flash also offers shorter latency, improving boot performance, which is critical for applications that need near-instantaneous access to stored data.

3D NOR flash offers an eight-fold density increase, achieving 512 Mb on a single die compared to 2D NOR’s maximum density capability of 512 Mb. It also employs the 200 MHz double transfer rate (DTR) to facilitate high-speed data transfer for AI applications that require rapid access to large amounts of data.

But while 3D NOR flash promises a new era of high-capacity embedded storage, it’s still years away from commercial availability on a wide scale. Especially when the large memory makers aren’t investing in NOR flash technology anymore. Furthermore, leading supplier Macronix plans to start sampling 3D NOR flash devices in the second half of 2026 and begin full-scale production in 2027.

Till then, the industry will have to deal with 2D NOR flash and its supply crunch. It’s a smaller memory segment compared to DRAM and NAND, but its surging demand in AI designs and a larger reshuffle in manufacturing capacity due to increasing demand for HBM make NOR flash also a memory technology to watch.

data.title: Imec to Open R&D Center in Qatar
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

DOHA, Qatar — Imec will open an R&D center in Qatar shortly, which will serve as the research institute’s hub in the GCC region. Supported by Invest Qatar and the Qatar Research, Development & Innovation Council (QRDI), imec’s new hub has already started hiring.

“Talent is extremely important,” Max Mirgoli, chief global development and venturing officer at imec, told EE Times. “For the last 40 years, we’ve been able to attract talent to imec [in Belgium and around the world], but recently, immigration rules have become more challenging.”

Engineers and academics from major sources of semiconductor talent, such as India, can apply for a U.S. H-1B visa, but the Trump administration has made it harder to get the H-1B visa and has made hiring H-1B workers more difficult and costly.

“This is a free gift to us, and to Qatar,” Mirgoli said. “[Qatar] is going to be a place where we can curate talent from the region.”

This includes semiconductor talent from countries like India, Turkey, Armenia, and Egypt, he said, noting that part of the deal is fast visa processing support from the Qatari government.

“[After university], the best place to train top engineers is imec,” Mirgoli said. “This is what we bring to [Qatar]. What we get is talent that couldn’t otherwise go to the U.S. or to Europe.”

University support

Since the pandemic, there is a new appreciation for the importance of semiconductors to all other industries, Mirgoli said. All countries are subject to geopolitical issues and national security concerns, such as data sovereignty, but in the GCC in particular, countries want to diversify their economies away from oil and develop industries, including personalized medicine, advanced automotive systems, and physical AI.

While building a huge cutting-edge foundry would be “barking up the wrong tree” for most countries, including GCC countries, supporting semiconductor R&D and chip-design efforts is within reach, Mirgoli said, especially since advanced EDA tools are lowering the barriers to entry for chip design.

Qatar is working hard on building a talent pipeline to support these fields. The government has supported the development of several universities that can offer deep-tech degrees, including Hamad Bin Khalifa University (HBKU) and Qatar University, as well as satellite campuses of U.S. institutions like Carnegie Mellon.

Imec already contributes to curriculums at HBKU and Carnegie Mellon and offers internships to students. The new R&D center will offer internships, Ph.D.s, and postdocs in a variety of topics. These topics will include advanced computer architectures for AI and AI for chip design.

“AI for chip design is a great training ground for talent, because they learn the newest methodology of designing a chip,” Mirgoli said.

Projects at the R&D center will also include using imec’s semiconductor expertise in applications like healthcare. Imec has decades of experience developing diagnostic therapies, but future projects at the new R&D center could include genomics and proteomics.

Attracting startups

Imec’s new GCC hub will also accelerate the development of semiconductor startups in the region by supporting them with imec’s advanced technology, plus prototyping and manufacturing facilities. Funding can be provided via the imec.xpand fund.

“A successful startup needs access to the market, access to capital, and access to talent,” Mirgoli said. “Access to talent is the weak leg of the equation.”

Qatar can provide access to the market, Mirgoli said, in a similar way to how the U.S. government buys technology from U.S. companies. Qatar can also provide capital for startups via its sovereign wealth fund, the QIA. Chip startups should consider making a base in the country to take advantage of these factors, he said.

“Startups can have many locations [thanks to remote working], many are already bi-coastal or European-U.S, so why not Qatar?” he said.

The GCC countries have different strategies for attracting startups, Mirgoli said, noting that some countries require a base in the country to get investment. Qatar’s sovereign wealth fund, the QIA, doesn’t tend to require this as part of its investment deals, he added. “[Qatar] focuses a lot on talent development because they realize it’s the best thing to do,” he said. “Why Qatar [for imec]? We looked at where there is a desire to learn, and where we can contribute the most, and that’s here.”

data.title: SiTime Bolsters Timing Portfolio with Renesas-Acquired Clocks
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

SiTime has consolidated its position as a pure-play precision timing specialist by acquiring Renesas’ timing assets in a deal valued at up to $3.2 billion. In what SiTime CEO Rajesh Vashist calls a major strategic step, the Santa Clara, California-based timing semiconductor supplier will complement its MEMS oscillators with Renesas’ clocking solutions.

It’s the largest acquisition in SiTime’s corporate history, and EE Times reported about the ongoing talks between SiTime and Renesas in early December. EE Times also reported that other companies, including Infineon and Texas Instruments, were exploring the possibility of acquiring Renesas’ timing components business.

In a recent interview with EE Times, Vashist acknowledged that SiTime is currently focusing on a $3 billion segment of the $11 billion timing market. By acquiring Renesas’ timing assets, the company aims to capture a larger share of the market while also raising its profile as a silicon timing specialist.

On the other hand, Renesas aims to sharpen its focus on embedded processors while also entering a strategic collaboration to incorporate SiTime’s timing content into its microcontrollers. After spending a decade as a serial acquirer, Renesas has finally started executing balance sheet discipline and honing strategic clarity.

SiTime’s timing takeaways

SiTime, a supplier of MEMS oscillators, will acquire Renesas’ clock portfolio, which the Japanese chipmaker inherited from the 2018 purchase of Integrated Device Technology (IDT). It includes clock generators, buffers, network synchronizers, and jitter attenuators.

These timing products, boasting a 30-year legacy of highly differentiated clocking solutions, serve over 10,000 customers. Moreover, nearly 75% of the revenue of these clocks comes from the AI data center and communication markets. The rest is spread between automotive and industrial markets.

Clocks generate reference signals that allow electronic circuits to operate without disruption. (Source: Renesas)

SiTime CEO Vashist expects that Renesas’ timing assets will boost his company’s clocking portfolio by more than 10×. Moreover, they will extend SiTime’s reach to the fastest-growing timing content in AI data centers, communications, and enterprise. “These applications are expected to represent more than 60% of SiTime’s revenue post-acquisition,” he added.

The timing assets acquired in this deal, anticipated to close by the end of 2026, are also expected to generate $300 million in their first year of operation after closing of the deal. So, they will nearly double SiTime’s sales, recording $326.7 million in 2025.

A new timing integration frontier

This deal isn’t merely about asset acquisition. The two companies are also partnering to integrate SiTime’s MEMS resonators into Renesas’ embedded processing solutions. SiTime’s Titan MEMS resonators will be combined as a bare die with Renesas’ MCU or SoC die in a single package.

That will eliminate the need for an external resonator on the system board, which, in turn, will simplify the design while saving cost and space. That’s a significant premise for many applications—including advanced driver assistance systems, AI data centers, industrial robots, and wearables—where performance, energy efficiency, and miniaturization are critical.

The integration of the MEMS resonator die with the MCU provides performance, energy efficiency, and miniaturization advantages. (Source: SiTime)

“One of SiTime’s strategies is to integrate our resonators into MCUs and power management ICs, among other SoCs, to provide size, performance, and power benefits to semiconductor companies,” Vashist said. And this arrangement with Renesas provides that specific venue, which the SiTime CEO calls a strategic collaboration.

According to Vashist, the outcome of this collaboration will be the first MCUs on the market that don’t need any external timing components to operate. He noted, however, that it will take a couple of years to design these MCUs, qualify them, and bring them to market.

This arrangement also brings Renesas CEO Hidetoshi Shibata onto the SiTime board. Furthermore, the tie-up is expected to offer SiTime exposure to Renesas’ timing customer base of cloud hyperscalers and AI servers, as well as automotive, communication, and enterprise designs.

data.title: Google Bets Big on Power Generation to Fuel AI
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Google’s parent Alphabet has reached a definitive agreement to acquire renewable energy developer Intersect Power for $4.75 billion, a transaction that signals a structural transformation in how Silicon Valley intends to power the AI era.

The deal, which includes taking on Intersect Power’s debt, makes Alphabet the first major tech company to bring electricity generation in-house. By owning a power utility, Google can secure energy for its data centers directly.

This acquisition marks a departure from the industry’s decade-long standard of signing Power Purchase Agreements, where companies contract for energy from third-party developers. Instead, Google is taking ownership of a 3.6-gigawatt pipeline of late-stage solar and wind projects, along with 3.1 gigawatt-hours of battery storage.

“Intersect has always been focused on bringing innovation to the industry, and we look forward to accelerating at scale as part of Google,” said Sheldon Kimber, founder and CEO of Intersect. “Modern infrastructure is the linchpin of American competitiveness in AI. We share Google’s conviction that energy innovation and community investment are the pillars of what must come next.”

Google’s new strategy comes as the U.S. electricity sector faces its biggest supply challenges in decades. These are caused by more EVs, increased domestic manufacturing, and the rapid growth of AI computing.

Gigawatt AI problem

The main reason for the deal is how much energy generative AI uses. For example, a ChatGPT query needs about 2.9 watt-hours, almost 10× more than a regular Google search. As Alphabet plans to double its capital spending to $185 billion in 2026 to support these technologies, finding enough electricity has become the main challenge.

“The energy system, I would say, globally, is no longer fit for purpose, for serving the demands of AI,” said Amanda Peterson Corio, Google’s global head of data center energy. “What we’re trying to do is really think proactively.”

By buying Intersect, Google gets control of a developer with projects ready to start and, importantly, spots already secured in interconnection queues. In many U.S. markets, it now takes five to seven years for new power projects to connect to the grid. By owning these projects, Google can focus on serving its data centers in places like Texas, instead of waiting for the general market to add capacity.

Oliver Kerr, managing director for North America at Aurora Energy Research, noted the strategic logic of the move. “Just as Google brought in-house some of the elements around chip design to reduce reliance on Nvidia, I think it makes sense that they’re also bringing the power-generation piece in-house.”

Divergent strategies among hyperscalers

While Google is buying a developer, its competitors are taking other approaches to the same energy challenge. Microsoft has focused on financial strategies and bringing back nuclear power. In September 2024, Microsoft signed a 20-year deal with Constellation Energy to restart Unit 1 of the Three Mile Island nuclear plant, which will add 835 megawatts of carbon-free power to the grid by 2028.

At the same time, Microsoft started a $30 billion global AI infrastructure investment partnership with BlackRock to fund energy projects. This helps deal with the reluctance of traditional lenders to finance such infrastructure.

Amazon is taking a different approach by buying distressed assets. Last month, it bought the 1.2-gigawatt Sunstone solar-plus-storage project from a bankrupt developer for $83 million. Like Google, Amazon wants to control energy assets directly, but it is focusing on buying individual projects instead of an entire development company.

Nuclear renaissance and small reactors

Besides renewables, the industry is investing heavily in nuclear energy to provide constant power, something wind and solar cannot do without large battery storage. Tech companies are betting on small modular reactors (SMRs), which can be built faster than traditional nuclear plants.

Comparative analysis of hyperscaler nuclear strategies

Source: Market Reports and Corporate Filings

Prior to the Intersect acquisition, Google had signed an agreement with Kairos Power to install 500 MW of SMR capacity by 2035, using a new salt-cooled design. Amazon has invested $500 million in X-energy to help deploy high-temperature gas-cooled reactors.

However, the timelines for these technologies remain long. “There’s no free lunch, there’s no magic wand that makes all the problems go away,” said Tim Latimer, CEO of Fervo Energy, a geothermal startup backed by Google. “That’s something I think Google internalized a lot sooner than the rest of their peers.”

Economics of power integration

Owning energy assets gives Google clear benefits but also brings new risks. By controlling the developer, Google can use the “Energy Park” model, placing data centers next to power sources. This setup can help avoid transmission congestion charges and maintain power quality. Sheldon Kimber stressed the need for this scale. “You don’t even get a seat at the table here without being a big, big player in physical infrastructure.”

However, this strategy also brings regulatory attention. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) recently rejected a plan to place an Amazon data center at a nuclear plant in Pennsylvania, saying it could shift transmission costs to others. This shows that tech companies can’t easily avoid the financial rules of the larger power grid.

Regulatory headwinds and consumer protection

The idea of tech giants controlling power generation has worried utilities and consumer advocates. In Ohio, regulators approved a “Large Load Tariff” that makes data centers pay for 85% of their contracted capacity, even if they don’t use it all. This protects residential customers from paying for upgrades that might not be needed.

Brad Smith, Microsoft’s president, tried to address these worries in January 2026 with a “Community First” pledge. The company promised to pay all extra costs for its electricity use and to give up property tax discounts in the communities where it operates.

Emerging regulatory frameworks for large loads

Source: AEP Ohio Tariff Filings and Georgia Power

Future outlook

As we approach 2030, the line between tech companies and power utilities will become less clear. The “hypergrid” model predicts that data centers will shift from being passive users to active grid participants, using the same AI tools to reduce computing during peak demand. Google has already shown this by using “carbon-intelligent computing” to pause non-urgent tasks when the grid is under stress, acting as a demand response resource.

Furthermore, Alphabet’s CEO Sundar Pichai said that even with these large investments, supply will have trouble keeping up, and he “still expects demand from its DeepMind research lab and from customers to outstrip the new computing power it can bring online”.

Buying Intersect Power shows that for the biggest tech companies, energy is now something to manage, not just buy. The key question for the AI era is whether this approach can overcome the limits of the aging U.S. electric grid.

See also:

Silicon Carbide Growth Pivots Around AI and Energy

Delivering Energy for Abundant AI Will Require New Playbook

Solving AI’s Power Struggle

data.title: Inside FAMES: How Europe Depends on RTO Collaboration
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

When the European Commission (EC) talks about semiconductor sovereignty, it doesn’t mean building a single major fab that does everything. Instead, the EC is betting on a distributed model: tightly linked pilot lines operated by research and technology organizations (RTOs), each contributing specialized process modules that industry can access early.

According to Anne Roule, head of the Semiconductor Fab division at CEA-Leti, collaboration among European RTOs is nothing new. Mechanisms are already in place, particularly around wafer processing, cleanroom operations, and shared manufacturing methodologies. For example, her organization may send wafers to another institute, such as imec, to carry out certain technology modules before being returned or passed along to another partner. She also noted that collaboration sometimes takes place at the design level, where RTOs jointly contribute to device or process development without necessarily exchanging wafers.

FAMES builds on this cooperative approach. One of five Chips Joint Undertaking pilot lines funded under Europe’s semiconductor strategy, it targets five technology pillars—advanced FD-SOI nodes, embedded nonvolatile memories, RF passives, 3D integration, and integrated power management—chosen because together they enable new chip architectures across edge AI, communications, automotive, and even NewSpace and cryogenic electronics.

To ensure these technologies don’t stay in the lab too long, FAMES has adopted an open access policy, which, according to Susana Bonnetier, Open Access chairperson for the FAMES pilot line at CEA-Leti, is one of the defining features of the project. “Every year, we launch an open call,” she said. “We tell users which technologies are ready, and they can access them through multi-project wafers, demonstrator results, or tailor-made projects.”

As the FAMES Pilot Line project coordinator and hosting site, CEA-Leti leads the common work of the project. It is also the main developer and integrator of the FD-SOI advanced nodes, embedded nonvolatile memories, RF passive components, 3D technologies, and sustainability activities. Other RTOs contribute in ways that take advantage of their strengths.

Tyndall: bringing power delivery onto the chip

Tyndall National Institute contributes one of the less visible—but increasingly critical—pieces of the puzzle: integrated power management. “Tyndall’s specialty is power management components, meaning on-silicon inductors, transformers, and PMICs,” Bonnetier explained. “They can fabricate and integrate inductors and transformers very close to the chip itself, so you can control power efficiently.”

Cian O’Mathuna, who leads power electronics research at Tyndall, framed the challenge in blunt terms. “If you look at high-performance computing and AI processors today, all the power is coming in laterally from the circuit board, and there are enormous losses,” he said.

Tyndall’s approach is to move magnetic components directly onto silicon, replacing bulky external inductors with dense arrays of micro-scale integrated magnetics. “The magnetics disappear onto the silicon,” O’Mathuna said. “That’s how we believe the power problem will be solved at these current densities.”

Within FAMES, that work complements FD-SOI’s low-power characteristics, enabling systems where power delivery is no longer a dominant inefficiency.

VTT: RF filters for crowded 6G spectrum

Meanwhile, VTT contributes primarily in RF and back-end-of-line memory technologies, with a strong focus on RF filters operating in future 5G and 6G frequency bands. “At higher frequencies, there’s a tight spacing between the different bands—and the signal bleeding from one band to another is a big challenge,” Karl-Magnus Persson, project manager at VTT, said. “To communicate reliably, you need very narrow, very sharp filters with low signal attenuation.”

VTT is developing RF filters using aluminum scandium nitride, a material that allows precise control of resonance frequencies. By tightly controlling layer thickness and composition, Persson said, VTT can target specific bands between roughly 7 and 15 GHz with clean edges.

Those filters pair naturally with FD-SOI, which is already well-suited for RF applications. “FD-SOI allows for efficient high-frequency operation, and it will be possible to assemble a full front-end system together with 3D integrated passive components being developed in parallel,” Persson said. “Together, these technologies create a very differentiating solution for communications systems.”

SAL: miniaturizing RF circulators

Another RF contribution comes from Silicon Austria Labs (SAL), one of the FAMES hosting sites. Bonnetier emphasized its role in miniaturizing RF circulators—a standard function in RF systems that has rarely been integrated with advanced nodes.

“Circulators exist today, but they’ve never been miniaturized to this extent for these frequency ranges,” Bonnetier said. “SAL is bringing deep expertise in circulators, and what’s innovative here is scaling them down for FD-SOI and 5G/6G applications.”

Jérémy Létang, senior scientist at SAL, said the goal is to make circulators compatible with the same dense integration as other RF passives, enabling compact, low-loss RF front ends suitable for next-generation RF communications.

Fraunhofer: embedded ferroelectric memories

Fraunhofer focuses on embedded nonvolatile memories, particularly ferroelectric RAM (FeRAM), integrated into the FD-SOI back end of line. “Fraunhofer is developing new nonvolatile memories based on several material systems and adapting them specifically to FD-SOI technology,” said Wenke Weinreich, deputy institute director at Fraunhofer. “The goal is to enable low-power in-memory computing concepts for AI accelerators.”

Because these memories sit close to logic, they reduce data movement—a major source of energy consumption. Weinreich noted that integrating FD-SOI with ferroelectric memories creates “a really good combination for ultra-low-power systems, which is exactly what edge AI needs.”

Imec: scaling, materials, and sustainability metrics

Imec also contributes in several domains, from advanced scaling and memory concepts to sustainability analysis. “None of this would be possible without bringing together complementary capabilities across Europe,” said Alessio Spessot, technical account director at imec. “We are not duplicating expertise—we are combining it.”

One distinctive imec contribution to FAMES is its environmental “E-score,” a method for quantifying the water, energy, and materials footprint of individual process steps. By applying the same metrics across pilot lines, imec aims to make sustainability measurable—and comparable—at the process-module level.

CEZAMAT-WUT: designing memories for hardware security and IMC

CEZAMAT-WUT, the Centre for Advanced Materials and Technologies of Warsaw University of Technology, will contribute its expertise in the design, modeling, fabrication, and characterization of nonvolatile memory devices, in particular OxRAM devices, notably in the in-memory computing and cybersecurity sectors.

“OxRAM devices are very well suited for hardware security applications because their intrinsic properties provide the entropy needed to generate unique cryptographic keys that are virtually impossible to replicate,” said Piotr Wisniewski, head of the Intelligent Semiconductor Systems Department at CEZAMAT-WUT.

Tying it all together—sharing wafers and best practices and accelerating lab-to-fab

While each RTO contributes distinct process blocks, the collaboration can only work if those blocks are physically combined. Wafers sent between sites are packed in specialized front-opening shipping boxes (FOSBs) and transported by carriers such as DHL or FedEx. Before a wafer can enter another cleanroom, it must pass contamination checks for particles and metallic residues. “Different fabs tolerate different contamination levels,” Roule explained. “So we send what we call witness wafers to verify compatibility, which allows us to qualify our cleanroom partners and facilitate wafer exchanges.”

These traveling wafers are known as “flying wafers”—and they do more than carry devices. “They force us to harmonize our protocols and learn how each other’s cleanrooms operate,” Roule said. Over time, that exchange of best practices helps homogenize operations across Europe’s RTO network.

For Europe, FAMES is less about a single pilot line than about proving a collaborative model. By sharing wafers, metrics, and practices across RTOs, the project aims to make advanced semiconductor technology both accessible and industrially credible—before it ever reaches a fab.

See also:

data.title: Fabless Startup Aheesa Tapes Out First Indian RISC-V Network SoC
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Indian fabless semiconductor startup Aheesa Digital Innovations has completed the tape-out of a domestically designed RISC-V-based broadband networking SoC, called VIHAAN-I, targeting optical fiber access networks used by telecom operators to deliver last-mile connectivity to homes and offices.

The chip is designed for gigabit passive optical network (GPON) and Ethernet passive optical network (EPON) optical network terminal applications and will be released as part of Aheesa’s Seshnag platform. UMC is manufacturing the chip.

In an exclusive interview with EE Times, Aheesa founder and CEO Sridharan Mani described VIHAAN-I as the first RISC-V-based broadband access SoC to be fully designed and developed in India. “VIHAAN-I is a 28-nm network SoC that integrates compute, broadband access, and system control on a single chip and is built around the CDAC Vega processor core based on the RISC-V architecture,” he said.

Aheesa Digital Innovations was established in 2021 by Mani and a team of co-founders, who include industry veterans with several decades of experience in firmware, semiconductors, and telecom. The company has received support under India’s design-linked incentive scheme (approved in 2023) and has worked with government-backed institutions including the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, the India Semiconductor Mission, and the Centre for Development of Advanced Computing.

“In a typical home or office, a fiber cable terminates in a white box, and inside that box there is a chip,” Mani said. “That chip, the board, and even the antennas usually come from outside India. What we are doing is replacing that chip with VIHAAN-I, which is designed and developed entirely by us.”

The SoC supports passive optical network (PON) architectures commonly used in fiber-to-the-home and fiber-to-the-office deployments. It interfaces with optical line terminals (OLTs) deployed at telecom operator facilities, converting optical signals into Ethernet and wireless data within customer premises equipment.

One OLT can support up to 128 optical network units, typically deployed in apartment buildings or residential clusters. “Each optical network unit can be built using the VIHAAN-I chip along with the Seshnag reference hardware platform,” Mani said.

“What differentiates this SoC is the use of an open instruction set architecture, support for recent Linux kernels, and newer memory and interface technologies compared with many legacy access devices,” he added.

Technical architecture of VIHAAN-I

Mani said the choice of an Indian-developed RISC-V core was driven primarily by security considerations. “Network is all about security,” he said. “You need to know every component that goes into it. If the core is coming from India, you know the security is already in place.”

Aheesa evaluated both the Vega and Shakti RISC-V cores and selected Vega based on familiarity at the time, adding that both cores are suitable for networking applications, according to Mani.

At the hardware level, the SoC includes DDR4 memory support and interfaces for SPI NOR flash, embedded multimedia card storage, and SD or microSD cards. For broadband access, it integrates an xPON interface and supports multiple Ethernet standards, including 10BASE-TE, 100BASE-TX, 100BASE-FX, and 1000BASE-T.

The device integrates multi-port Gigabit Ethernet and direct optical fiber connectivity for access network deployments.

Block Diagram of VIHAAN-I (Source: Aheesa Digital Innovations)

High-speed expansion is supported through a four-lane PCIe interface, with support for M.2 and mini-PCIe connectivity. Additional high-speed peripheral expansion is supported through integrated PCIe and USB interfaces.

Peripheral interfaces include multiple inter-integrated circuit controllers, synchronous serial port interfaces, and support for on-board devices like electrically erasable programmable read-only memory and sensors. The design also integrates native voice support, including a plain old telephone service interface and a dedicated voice path, for deployment in access gateways.

Standard bring-up and debug interfaces are included for system validation and deployment.

The SoC implements GPON and EPON protocols along with standard networking functions, such as Ethernet switching, routing, firewall services, VPNs, dynamic host configuration protocol, and domain name system services. Mani said the architecture also supports optional fifth-generation cellular backhaul for redundancy.

“If the fiber is cut, the user does not need to panic,” he said. “You can still have a fifth-generation backhaul in the same box.”

Mani said VIHAAN-I has undergone both functional verification and system-level validation, including testing on FPGA platforms and interoperability testing with industry-standard optical line terminals. Public demonstrations of the technology have also been conducted.

In terms of intellectual property, Mani said roughly 60% of the SoC consists of standard licensed components, such as memory controllers and interface blocks. The remaining elements, including GPON and EPON implementations, network hardware accelerators, and cybersecurity functions, are developed internally. Mani said only a small number of companies globally, including Aheesa, have developed complete GPON and EPON access SoC with integrated cryptographic functionality.

Translating tech wonder to sales figures

Aheesa plans to offer VIHAAN-I as merchant silicon, rather than limiting it to in-house products. The startup is targeting OEMs, ODMs, system integrators, and distributors supplying access devices to telecom operators as its primary customers.

“Think of it like a processor and a reference platform,” Mani said. “Different vendors will build different products on top of it.”

Customers can license the chip alone, adopt the Seshnag reference platform, or procure finished end products for specific market segments. Mani said the business model allows full white labeling, with licensing and royalty arrangements for the underlying IP.

Mani said this tape-out is the first in a planned family of networking SoCs, with a roadmap extending to 2032. Future versions are expected to support higher-speed optical standards, including 10-gigabit passive optical networks, along with Wi-Fi 7 integration. Mani said later variants may also include AI accelerators to analyze network traffic at the edge.

“What I am interested in is abnormal traffic that needs strong AI capability at the edge,” Mani said.

Mani added that the long-term goal is to take an Indian-designed broadband access platform to global markets. “When you build technology for your own country, it is more special, but the idea is not only India. The idea is to go global,” he said, hinting at the company’s future plans.

data.title: IBM, Synopsys Move Toward 1.4-nm Node with Heat-Modeling Tech
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

IBM is aiming for the 1.4-nm node thanks to new heat-modeling tech it developed with Synopsys and the support of U.S military research agency DARPA. The companies told EE Times they will share the technology with chipmaking partners as the 2-nm node ramps up. TSMC and other chipmakers like Samsung started 2-nm manufacturing last year.

IBM’s move comes after the company led the chipmaking industry with the announcement of the world’s first 2-nm chips in 2021. IBM dropped out of commercial chipmaking decades ago, yet it remains a major player with a tech stack that includes fabrication and advanced packaging.

As part of its latest effort, IBM Research developed a new machine learning (ML) tool with Ansys, now part of Synopsys, in a project supported by the U.S. DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency). IBM announced the results under DARPA’s Thermonat project, short for Thermal Design of Nanoscale Transistors. IBM modeled thermal behavior of chips down to the atomic level.

As transistor nodes shrink, heat becomes a bigger problem. AI is driving increased chip power density and generating more heat. IBM said it trained the ML software on its stores of semiconductor data, achieving prediction accuracy within 1 degree Celsius, tens of thousands of times faster than the next best simulation tools.

“We expect that chips adopting this technology will appear during this 2-nm node technology cycle,” Russ Robison, EDA lead architect at IBM Research, told EE Times. “It will be a direct requirement for performance at 1.4-nm node and below. Data centers and high-performance applications (AI) are the first space, but cell phones will follow too.”

The ability to accurately model heat sources in chips can provide a powerful tool to engineers who are designing cooling systems for new chips, according to Timothy Chainer, a subsystem cooling and integration expert at IBM Research. “When designing chip layout, they can produce a thermally aware layout,” he said.

DARPA needed the model to predict heat properties within a 1% margin of accuracy. The U.S. agency was looking for solutions 100× faster than the current state-of-the-art tech—building a physical model of a new device to gather thermal data. Robison and his colleagues were able to model within 1 degree Celsius of experimental data, and they did it 50,000× faster than current methods.

“The Thermonat teams pushed the boundaries of what is possible in chip-scale thermal prediction,” said Yogendra Joshi, Thermonat program manager at DARPA, in a press release. “By connecting fundamental physics with design-ready tools, they created capabilities that can accelerate innovation for both national-security applications and the broader semiconductor industry.”

Chipmakers need tools that can predict thermally driven failures before investing years and hundreds of millions of dollars in fabrication. Yet, existing commercial modeling tools have not been able to fully capture nanoscale heat flow, while emerging atomic-level methods may be accurate but often require weeks or months to run, making them impractical for real-world design cycles, DARPA said in a press release.

For semiconductors today, the difference in performance for a heat-optimized design versus a non-thermal optimized design is between 5-15%, Robison added. From the development side, the technology needs to take into account the thermal performance of the whole “client” application, including circuit design and use case, he noted. From the design/usage side, thermal behavior is a big limiter of potential performance/margin, and having better thermal feedback is an improvement, Robison said.

IBM is using the methodology to consider overall 3D-IC chip use cases.

“Because of the speed and capability, we can model whole 3D-IC examples in the same way, just with a little longer run time,” Robison said. “The current thermal knowledge is heading into IBM’s chiplet and advanced-packaging technologies and assembly design kits.”

Tech transfer

IBM declined to name the chipmaking partners it will share the tech with. The company is working with Japan’s startup foundry Rapidus to start production at the 2-nm node in 2027. In August last year, Rapidus began prototyping 2-nm gate-all-around (GAA) transistors at its new facility, a key step toward starting production in 2027.  IBM also uses Samsung as a foundry supplier.

Most of the new developments will remain in-house for use in IBM projects and those of IBM clients. A team working on transistors at IBM is adopting the tech, while another is developing future 3D-IC devices. IBM will also use the tech for chip packaging and heterogeneous integration.

Synopsys and IBM project

Ansys, now part of Synopsys, is contributing two key technologies to the Thermonat project with IBM, Synopsys fellow Norman Chang told EE Times.

“The first is a reduced-order modeling approach that enables fast self-heating calculations for 2-nm GAA transistor designs,” Chang said. “The second is a machine-learning–based thermal solver that uses a per-tile activation methodology combined with Fourier neural operator modeling, delivering up to a 1,000× speed-up without sacrificing accuracy for designs with more than one-million transistors. Our intent is to continue maturing both technologies with widened capabilities for future release.”

An ML technique called a Fourier neural operator, which employs a neural network training format, aided the development of the reduced-order modeling (ROM). The Fourier neural operator is particular to machine learning for solving partial differential equation matrices, so it was uniquely suited to this scenario, Robison said.

A ROM is a simplified version of a complex, high-fidelity mathematical model, created to cut computational costs while retaining essential behaviors, enabling faster analysis and real-time simulations where full models are too slow or resource intensive. A ROM trades a little accuracy for massive speed, making complex engineering problems more manageable.

The tool can help squeeze more performance out of chips, Chainer said. Improved cooling solutions can allow higher chip power at the same operating temperature to enable increased computational performance. Alternatively, more effective cooling solutions can enable lower chip operating temperature to cut chip power consumption and improve efficiency.

Synopsys is gathering evaluations of the new self-heating ROM and the fast ML-based thermal solver.

“The ML-based solver supports both static and transient workloads in digital and analog circuits, delivering 1,000× or greater speed-up,” Chang said. “It’s another example of how Synopsys is applying ML and AI to accelerate all stages of the design flow. We look forward to sharing more about this at DesignCon 2026.”

data.title: What TI’s Acquisition of Silicon Labs Stands For?
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Texas Instruments, a leading supplier of analog and power chips, is bolstering its embedded processing capabilities by acquiring Silicon Laboratories at a time when the IoT world is remaking itself with edge AI. Silicon Labs’s embedded processors, specializing in wireless connectivity and hardware security, are expected to bolster TI’s presence in IoT and edge AI designs.

TI’s CEO, Haviv Ilan, acknowledged embedded processing as the primary driver of this acquisition. TI will acquire the Austin, Texas-based specialist of mixed-signal chips for approximately $7.5 billion; it’s TI’s largest acquisition since it bought National Semiconductor for $6.5 billion in 2011.

It’s also another analog-meets-digital story. In a way, it seems a redux of Infineon’s 2020 acquisition of Cypress Semiconductor, which allowed the German chipmaker to venture into the IoT-centric connectivity world that’s gradually advancing toward the edge AI-enabled automotive, consumer, and industrial designs.

According to industry insiders, though TI has a significant presence in the embedded processing space, its embedded solutions have a long way to go in IoT silicon and related software tools. On the other hand, Silicon Labs is known for robust software development kits (SDKs) and development tools for its wireless connectivity and hardware security silicon.

At CES 2026, Silicon Labs showcased a simplified SDK for Zephyr, the go-to open RTOS for connected embedded systems. The SDK, which offers a portable, production-grade alternative to proprietary kernels, streamlines RTOS adoption for next-generation IoT designs.

The fab angle

Besides Silicon Labs’ embedded processors—which have steadily evolved from MCUs and MPUs into specialized connectivity SoCs—TI’s recent fab buildup could be another driver behind this deal. The new 300-mm fab in Sherman, Texas, bolsters TI’s internally owned manufacturing network, which could now encompass Silicon Labs’ portfolio of 1,200 wireless connectivity solutions.

“The transaction is expected to generate ~$450 million in annual manufacturing and operational synergies within three years post-close,” notes TI’s press release announcing this acquisition. The press release also indicates that TI’s process technologies, including the 28-nm node, are optimized for Silicon Labs’ wireless connectivity portfolio.

Still, IoT and its transformation with edge AI solutions seem to lay the ground for this $7.5 billion deal. TI, a supplier of analog and mixed-signal chips for automotive, consumer, and industrial applications, has to get its act together in the newly energized world of IoT, thanks to edge AI solutions that seem to have reached an inflection point in 2025.

The edge AI imperative

AI is another defining factor in this deal. No company, analog or digital, could afford to miss the AI boat. At CES 2026, Silicon Labs showcased Bluetooth Channel Sounding and wireless motor control solutions aided by AI content. Moreover, the company has recently launched a suite of modular software tools with AI augmentation, aiming to incorporate connected intelligence into embedded IoT developments.

Next, it has equipped its Wi-Fi 6 and Bluetooth low energy (BLE) combo processor with an AI/ML accelerator. The dedicated hardware accelerator is optimized for energy-efficient ML inference, offloading the Arm Cortex-M4 application MCU. The on-device AI/ML inference capabilities include executing time series data processing ML models such as predictive maintenance, environmental monitoring, anomaly detection, voice and audio detection, and low-resolution vision use cases.

The AI boom, which unleashed a wave of acquisitions over the past couple of years, has finally reached the edge. TI’s deal could be a testament to the viability of edge AI solutions and the arrival of a new class of AI-native silicon now aligned with modern ML workflows. In addition to the embedded processing portfolio, the acquisition of Silicon Labs is likely to complement TI’s ongoing work on edge AI silicon and related software development kits.

data.title: Positron’s $230M Funding Led By Financial Trading Firms
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Doha, Qatar — Data center AI inference chip startup Positron has raised a huge $230-million series B to develop and deploy its AI ASIC, Asimov. The round was led by strategic investors, including Arena Private Wealth and Jump Trading, both Chicago-based financial trading firms and potential Positron customers.

The 34-month-old startup has reached a post-money valuation of over $1 billion, making it one of an increasing number of AI chip unicorns.

Positron CEO Mitesh Agrawal told EE Times that the large raise was due to “insane” inbound interest. The company operates in a very capital-efficient way, he said, having spent only $38 million to date. The company has already received purchase orders in excess of that amount, he noted.

“[The large raise] is a way for us to go on offense,” Agrawal said. “When we are going to go this big, both on the supply side and the vendor side of the ecosystem, bypassing CoWoS and HBM, we have to position ourselves to show that we can follow through. We also want to give our customers confidence that we can scale out production.”

While Agrawal is confident in Positron’s architecture and technology, the extra funding also gives the company “multiple shots at a go-to-market strategy,” he said. “More capital allows us to cater to different workloads, to go to different types of customer bases. And to have a longer time in the market to pitch our silicon.”

“When the market offers you capital, you take it,” he added. “You figure out a way that it is good for all of you, but you take it. Times may turn, and it could get challenging, even with an amazing product, so there’s an element of pragmatism too.”

Other new investors include the Qatar Investment Authority (QIA) and climate-focused fund Helena.

“[Climate] is a new angle for us,” Agrawal said. “I did a pitch at a conference around performance per Watt efficiency and how that can be impactful in terms of using existing air-cooled data centers, and that led to a conversation. We realized there is potential interest from that [climate] perspective as well [as performance and performance per dollar].”

FPGA-based

Positron is working on hardware acceleration for transformer inference in the data center. The company’s first-generation product, Atlas, uses FPGAs—specifically, the Agilex-7M with HBM and DDR5. The company’s secret sauce is in its method of achieving extremely high memory bandwidth utilization (93% for HBM); transformer inference is memory-bound, but despite FPGAs’ relatively low FLOPS, better utilizing memory bandwidth can produce tokens faster than current-generation GPUs.

The company is working on a second-generation product, a multi-die ASIC it calls Asimov. Asimov will use LPDDR memory (no HBM), but the company’s ability to get close to the theoretical peak memory bandwidth means they don’t need to rely on HBM to produce fast tokens, Positron CTO Thomas Sohmers told EE Times.

Asimov’s compute elements are an evolution of Atlas’s, with Arm cores added and networking expanded. Asimov is set to have 2 TB of LPDDR5x memory capacity; four Asimovs will be hosted in each air-cooled server, Titan.

Positron has a joint development agreement with Credo Semiconductor on the Weaver memory fanout gearbox chiplet, which enables high-bandwidth memory connectivity for Asimov. Weaver achieves 2 Tbits per millimeter of beachfront at 1 pJ per bit using Credo’s 112 G very short reach (VSR) serdes on one side and LPDDR PHYs on the other. Asimov will use 20 Weaver chiplets. As the design is chiplet-based, Asimov will get a mini-tock when Credo releases the LPDDR6 version of Weaver, Sohmers said, without needing a respin.

Positron has grown its team to 50 in the last six months and will grow to around 100 by the end of 2026. The startup is handling all its ASIC design work in-house, preferring to keep close control of the final design and mask set, knowing that iterations in the final stages of the process can be critical, Sohmers said. (While the majority of Positron’s technical team are FPGA experts, many in Asimov’s hardware team of 22 also have significant ASIC design experience, Sohmers said.) Moving forward, Positron intends to continue commercializing an FPGA-based version of future products, followed by an ASIC version in a model based on Intel’s famous tick-tock cadence. This means the company can get to market faster and get customer feedback faster, Sohmers said, while being better able to try new ideas. The target is an annual release cadence from now on, he added.

Financial trading

Lead investor Jump Trading had come to Positron as a customer, but was so impressed that it became an investor.

“For the workloads we care about, the bottlenecks are increasingly memory and power—not theoretical compute,” said Jump Trading CTO Alex Davies. “In our testing, Positron Atlas delivered roughly 3× lower end-to-end latency than a comparable H100-based system on the inference workloads we evaluated, in an air-cooled, production-ready footprint with a supply chain we can plan around. The deeper we went, the more we agreed with Positron’s roadmap—Asimov and the Titan systems—as a memory-first platform built for future workloads. We invested because Positron combines traction today with a roadmap that can reshape the cost curve and capabilities for inference.”

Aside from speed, companies like Jump Trading are looking for low power as they typically deploy both in exchanges and in their own data centers, Sohmers said, noting that on-prem racks can typically handle 7-15 kW per rack. Positron’s Atlas FPGA cards need 150-200 W each.

Financial trading companies do care about cost, Sohmers said, since a big customer in this space might be spending hundreds of millions on compute every year in a mix of cloud compute for R&D/algorithm development and on-prem hardware for deployment.

“These types of companies spend tens of millions on ASIC development for very low volume chips, but they are also responsible for the biggest market growth segment in the FPGA space,” Sohmers said.

Because their strategies and algorithms are proprietary, they generally want to keep ASIC development in-house.

“They can recoup their hardware investments quickly, meaning they can work with refresh cycles of a year or less,” Sohmers said.

According to Sohmers, Jump Trading was particularly impressed with what he calls “extreme ease of use.”

“Since we’re transformer-specific, they were able to evaluate [their workloads on] our technology remotely in a day,” Sohmers said. “An on-prem deployment they thought would take a couple of months, they managed to get up and running in weeks.”

Jump Trading welcomed the opportunity to work more closely with Positron on next-generation hardware, since visibility into their hardware roadmap can inform algorithm development. They have unique access to the program at the lower levels of Positron’s stack today, Sohmers said, noting that Positron is contemplating making these levels available to other customers.

The first deployment with Jump Trading is really a small test deployment, Agrawal said.

“This is a great market for us to find a footing,” Agrawal said. “There is specificity in the workload, but if you can show that [there’s a synergy with your architecture], there is plenty of stickiness.” 

Asimov is due to tape out toward the end of the third quarter, with samples coming at the end of the first quarter of 2027.

data.title: Manufacturing Sector Returns to Growth in January
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

U.S. manufacturing activity expanded in January for the first time in a year, ending a 12-month streak of contraction. The rebound marked a significant shift for the industrial sector, which had struggled through 26 months of contraction before a brief reprieve last year. As the manufacturing engine restarted, the overall U.S. economy continued its 15th consecutive month of expansion.

The latest report from the Institute for Supply Management (ISM) indicates a broad-based recovery, with demand surging and production ramping up to meet it. However, supply executives remain cautious regarding inflationary pressures and trade uncertainties.

Susan Spence, chair of the ISM Manufacturing Business Survey Committee, reported that the Manufacturing PMI registered 52.6% in January. This figure represents a 4.7-percentage-point increase from the 47.9% recorded in December. A reading above 50% signifies expansion in the manufacturing sector.

Spence noted that “in January, U.S. manufacturing activity returned to expansion territory, with improvements in all five subindexes that make up the PMI (New Orders, Production, Employment, Supplier Deliveries, and Inventories).” She added that the current PMI reading corresponds to a 1.7% annualized increase in real gross domestic product (GDP).

Demand, output, and employment show volatility

Demand measures surged at the start of the year. The New Orders Index jumped into expansion territory, rising to 57.1%, an increase of 9.7 percentage points from December. This represents the highest reading since February 2022. Spence observed that “three demand indicators (the New Orders, Backlog of Orders, and New Export Orders indexes) are in expansion, and the Customers’ Inventories Index remains in ‘too low’ territory, contracting at a faster rate.”

Spence cautioned that while these are positive signs, they are “tempered by commentary citing that January is a reorder month after the holidays, and some buying appears to be to get ahead of expected price increases due to ongoing tariff issues.”

For output, the Production Index expanded for the third consecutive month, registering 55.9%, a 5.2-percentage-point increase from December. Of the six largest manufacturing industries, five registered growth in January, including Transportation Equipment and Computer & Electronic Products.

The Employment Index improved significantly but remained in contraction territory at 48.1%, up 3.3 percentage points from the previous month. Spence pointed out that “66% of panelists still indicate that managing headcount is the norm at their companies as opposed to hiring.” She noted that companies continued to focus on accelerating staff reductions due to uncertain demand, with layoffs and unfilled positions remaining the primary management strategies.

Trade friction and costs influence the supply chain

Inputs, defined as supplier deliveries, inventories, prices, and imports, tightened in January. The Supplier Deliveries Index registered 54.4%, indicating slower delivery performance for the second consecutive month.

Spence noted that this slowing is typical as the economy improves and customer demand increases. The Inventories Index remained in contraction at 47.6%, though it rose 1.9 percentage points from December.

The Prices Index remained in expansion for the 16th straight month, inching up to 59%. Spence noted that “the Prices Index reading continues to be driven by increases in steel and aluminum prices that impact the entire value chain, as well as tariffs applied to many imported goods.” Consequently, the Imports Index recovered from contraction to register 50%, reaching a point of stability after falling sharply the prior month.

Outlook improves as backlog expands

The report showed that the scope of the manufacturing recovery widened significantly in January. Only 20% of the sector’s GDP contracted during the month, a sharp improvement from the 85% reported in December. Furthermore, the percentage of manufacturing GDP in “strong contraction”—defined as a PMI of 45% or lower—dropped to 12% from 43%.

The Backlog of Orders Index registered 51.6%, returning to growth after 39 months of contraction. Despite the improved data, respondent comments highlighted ongoing anxiety regarding government policy. A respondent in the Transportation Equipment sector noted that while they hope the second half of 2026 starts a turnaround, “it’s hard to set a strategy on hope.”

Others cited specific headwinds regarding trade policy. Executives in the Computer & Electronic Products industry warned that “another round of emotionally charged tariffs seems imminent,” while respondents in Machinery noted that geopolitical tensions are fueling “anti-American” buyer sentiment.
